title: Listening - Part 4: Buying an Old Property to Renovate
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people talking about their experience of buying and restoring a derelict building

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’ve always been a bit of a romantic, and [21]the idea of saving a piece of local history from falling down completely was a major draw for me[/21]. I bought a Victorian gatehouse. It was a ruin. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the amazing community of local craftsmen I’ve met during the process[/26]. They’ve shared so much knowledge about traditional building techniques that I never would have learned otherwise. It’s made me feel much more connected to the town and its heritage. || Speaker 2: We were looking for a way to get a large family home on a limited budget. [22]Buying a derelict barn allowed us to create a space that we simply could never have afforded if it were already finished[/22]. It was a financial necessity. However, [27]the biggest challenge was the physical exhaustion of working a full-time job and then spending every evening and weekend on site[/27]. There were times when I thought we’d never finish, but seeing the kids in their own rooms now makes it all worth it. || Speaker 3: I’m an architect, and [23]I took on a derelict industrial unit because I wanted a project where I could fully express my own design ideas without any client interference[/23]. It was a creative challenge. [28]What I found most difficult, though, was the constant battle with the local planning department over my unconventional materials[/28]. I had to spend months justifying every single decision, which was incredibly draining and delayed the project by nearly a year. || Speaker 4: I’d recently retired and needed a project to stay active. [24]I bought a small, abandoned chapel primarily because I wanted to learn new practical skills like masonry and carpentry[/24]. I didn't want to just sit in a chair all day. [29]The most rewarding outcome has been the massive boost in my own self-reliance[/29]. I now know how to fix a roof and plumb a sink, things I used to have to pay a fortune for. It’s given me a huge sense of accomplishment and independence. || Speaker 5: My husband and I were living in a tiny city flat and [25]we bought an old farmhouse because we desperately needed to escape the urban noise and find some peace[/25]. We wanted a project that would force us to slow down. It’s been a long road, but [30]the most significant change has been the improvement in our overall mental well-being[/30]. Living in a house that you’ve physically built with your own hands gives you a sense of security and belonging that I never felt in the city. It’s truly our home now.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to buy a derelict property.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to save a piece of local history from falling down.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions it was a financial necessity to get a large home on a limited budget.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted a project to express his own design ideas without interference.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to stay active in retirement and learn new practical skills.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to escape urban noise and find peace through a project.

options.A: the desire to live in a larger property for less
options.B: a wish to preserve a historical structure
options.C: the hope of making a quick financial profit
options.D: to be closer to family members
options.F: to create a space for a new business
options.G: the need for a tranquil environment

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions about their experience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions meeting a community of local craftsmen and feeling connected.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 highlights the physical exhaustion of working evenings and weekends.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions the constant battle with the local planning department.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 notes a boost in self-reliance and the ability to do repairs himself.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 mentions a significant improvement in their overall mental well-being.

options.A: a realization that they had overspent
options.B: a positive effect on their psychological state
options.C: the difficulty of finding suitable materials
options.D: a newfound sense of practical independence
options.E: the pleasure of discovering hidden features
options.F: the toll taken by the heavy workload
options.H: the value of the social links they forged
